events.text: The patient is a 48-year-old female named Margaret Okonkwo. She is of Nigerian descent, born in Lagos and residing in the United States for 20 years. She works as a licensed practical nurse. She has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril, type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with metformin, and a prior diagnosis of myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) with refractory cytopenia with multilineage dysplasia (RCMD) identified 18 months prior, for which she was observed without treatment. She is a never-smoker and consumes alcohol rarely. She has no known drug allergies.

At age 48 years, the patient had a diagnosis of AML with myelodysplasia-related changes (AML-MRC), per 2022 WHO classification, corresponding to Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) (SEER site code 9861/3), arising from antecedent MDS. Presenting AJCC/pathologic stage: not applicable (leukemia). Peripheral blood WBC at diagnosis: 84,000/uL (leukocytosis). Hemoglobin: 7.1 g/dL (severe anemia). Platelets: 28,000/uL (severe thrombocytopenia). Presenting symptoms included fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, gingival bleeding, easy bruising, and 2-week history of low-grade fevers. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed 62% myeloblasts. Cytogenetics showed normal female karyotype (46,XX) in 18/20 metaphases with no complex abnormalities. FLT3-ITD mutation detected with high allelic ratio (VAF 0.82, ITD insert length 39 bp). Auer rods present on peripheral smear. LDH elevated at 890 U/L. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY; Primary site: BONE MARROW (C421.9); Histology: Acute myeloid leukemia, NOS (9861/3).

At age 48 years, the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate performed at diagnosis. Biopsy showed a hypercellular marrow (95% cellularity) with 62% CD34-positive myeloblasts by flow cytometry immunophenotyping. Blasts expressed CD33+, CD117+, CD13+, HLA-DR+, CD34+, CD123+, partial CD7+. Myeloperoxidase (MPO) positive in a subset. Erythroid and megakaryocytic lineages showed dysplastic morphology consistent with underlying MDS. Reticulin fibrosis: MF-1. Immunohistochemistry: CD34 highlights blasts, MPO positive. Cytogenetics (karyotype): 46,XX [18/20], with 2 metaphases showing del(7q) (residual MDS-related clone). FISH panel: FLT3-ITD positive (high allelic ratio). NPM1: wild-type. CEBPA: wild-type. IDH1/IDH2: negative. TP53: wild-type by sequencing. Pathologic diagnosis: Acute myeloid leukemia with myelodysplasia-related changes.

At age 48 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing (NGS) performed for acute myeloid leukemia based on a specimen obtained from bone marrow aspirate at diagnosis. A 500-gene hematologic malignancy panel was used. Actionable alterations: FLT3 p.ITD (internal tandem duplication, exon 14-15 junction, allelic ratio 0.82, VAF 82%) - HIGH ALLELIC RATIO, targetable with midostaurin/gilteritinib. Additional somatic mutations: DNMT3A p.R882H (VAF 44%) - epigenetic regulator, poor prognostic association; SRSF2 p.P95H (VAF 38%) - splicing factor mutation, associated with MDS-related changes; ASXL1 p.G646Wfs*12 (VAF 41%) - chromatin remodeling, associated with MDS/AML-MRC; SF3B1: wild-type; TET2: wild-type; NPM1: wild-type; CEBPA: wild-type; IDH1: wild-type; IDH2: wild-type; RUNX1 p.R204Q (VAF 35%) - transcription factor, associated with MDS-related AML; WT1: wild-type; NRAS: wild-type; KRAS: wild-type; KIT: wild-type; TP53: wild-type; PTPN11: wild-type; CBL: wild-type; EZH2: wild-type; STAG2: wild-type; RAD21: wild-type; SMC1A: wild-type; PHF6: wild-type; BCOR: wild-type; BCORL1: wild-type; KDM6A: wild-type. Copy number alterations: del(7q) detected (partial, low clone). Fusions: none detected (FLT3-ITD confirmed, not a fusion). Microsatellite instability: stable (MSS). Tumor mutational burden: 4.2 mutations/Mb (low). Risk stratification per ELN 2022: ADVERSE (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio + DNMT3A + ASXL1 + SRSF2 + RUNX1 co-mutations; AML-MRC features). Recommendation: allogeneic stem cell transplant in first remission if CR achieved.

At age 48 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast performed at staging workup prior to induction chemotherapy. Findings: Mild splenomegaly (spleen 14.2 cm craniocaudal). No hepatomegaly. No lymphadenopathy (mediastinal, hilar, or abdominal). No pleural effusions. No pericardial effusion. Lungs: clear bilaterally without consolidation or masses. No lytic or blastic bony lesions. No soft tissue masses or organomegaly suggesting extramedullary AML (chloroma). Impression: Mild splenomegaly consistent with known leukemia. No evidence of extramedullary disease. Radiology assessment: cancer present (bone marrow disease, leukemia).

At age 48 years, prior to induction chemotherapy, the patient had a 2D echocardiogram performed for cardiac clearance. Findings: Normal left ventricular size and systolic function. Ejection fraction 62%. No wall motion abnormalities. Normal diastolic function. No valvular disease of hemodynamic significance. No pericardial effusion. Impression: Normal cardiac function. Cleared for anthracycline-based chemotherapy.

At age 48 years, the patient was admitted to the inpatient hematology unit and initiated on curative-intent 7+3 induction chemotherapy plus midostaurin for AML with FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio), AML-MRC, ELN adverse risk. Regimen: Cytarabine 200 mg/m2/day continuous IV infusion on days 1-7 (total dose per cycle: ~2800 mg/m2); Daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV push on days 1, 2, and 3; Midostaurin 50 mg orally twice daily on days 8-21 (FLT3 inhibitor added per RATIFY trial protocol). Supportive care: allopurinol for tumor lysis prophylaxis (held after day 3 as WBC responded); antimicrobial prophylaxis with fluconazole, acyclovir, and levofloxacin; aggressive IV hydration; anti-emetics (ondansetron, lorazepam); blood product transfusion support (pRBCs and platelets as needed). Treatment intent: curative; bridge to allogeneic stem cell transplant in CR1 given adverse-risk disease.

At age 48 years (approximately day 10 of induction), the patient developed febrile neutropenia (temperature 38.9 degrees C, ANC < 100/uL). Blood cultures drawn. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics initiated with cefepime 2g IV q8h. Subsequently, blood cultures grew Escherichia coli (1/2 bottles) sensitive to cefepime. Antibiotics continued for 14 days with resolution of fever by day 5 of antibiotic therapy. Additional adverse events during induction: Grade 3 mucositis (WHO grade 3) requiring morphine PCA for pain control and TPN for 8 days; Grade 2 nausea and vomiting managed with combination antiemetics; Grade 3 thrombocytopenia requiring platelet transfusions (2-3 times per week); Grade 3 anemia requiring pRBC transfusions (Hgb maintained above 8 g/dL); Grade 1 transaminase elevation (ALT peak 78 U/L, self-limited). No midostaurin dose reduction required. No cardiac toxicity.

At age 48 years (day 21 of induction, count nadir assessment), the patient had an inpatient oncology assessment. She remained hospitalized with persistent neutropenia (ANC 0/uL). Mucositis improving. Fever resolved. Tolerating oral intake. Repeat peripheral blood showed no circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy deferred to day 28-30 per protocol given expected hypoplasia. Plan: continue supportive care, await count recovery. Midostaurin continued through day 21. Overall clinical trajectory: anticipated aplasia, no concerning features for primary refractory disease at this time.

At age 48 years (day 30 post-induction, bone marrow re-staging biopsy), the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate performed. WBC had recovered to 1,800/uL, ANC 1,200/uL. Biopsy findings: Hypocellular marrow (25% cellularity) with regenerative trilineage hematopoiesis. Myeloblasts by morphology: less than 2%. No Auer rods identified. Flow cytometry: No aberrant CD34+ blast population detected; blasts less than 1% of total cellularity. Cytogenetics: 46,XX [20/20] - no del(7q) clone detected. Morphologic complete remission (CR) confirmed per ELN 2022 criteria. MRD assessment by 8-color flow cytometry: NEGATIVE (sensitivity threshold 0.01%; no leukemia-associated immunophenotype detected). FLT3-ITD by PCR: not detected (below assay sensitivity). Pathologic diagnosis: Complete remission with MRD negativity.

At age 48 years (approximately 5 weeks after induction start), the patient had an outpatient oncology clinic visit following discharge from inpatient hospitalization. She was discharged home 3 days after confirming CR. CBC: WBC 3,200/uL, Hgb 10.8 g/dL, platelets 142,000/uL - counts recovering well. No active bleeding or infection. Performance status ECOG 1. Midostaurin 50 mg BID continued as maintenance during consolidation planning phase. Discussion: Given ELN adverse-risk disease (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A/ASXL1/SRSF2/RUNX1 co-mutations, AML-MRC), allogeneic stem cell transplant (alloSCT) in CR1 is strongly recommended per current guidelines. HLA typing initiated. Referred to bone marrow transplant (BMT) team. Sibling HLA typing: no matched sibling identified. Unrelated donor search initiated via National Marrow Donor Program (NMDP/Be The Match). Cancer present: NO. Responding: YES (MRD-negative CR1).

At age 48 years (approximately 6 weeks post-induction start), the patient was admitted for consolidation chemotherapy cycle 1 while awaiting unrelated donor identification and transplant logistics. Regimen: High-dose cytarabine (HiDAC) 3 g/m2 IV over 3 hours every 12 hours on days 1, 3, and 5 (6 doses total per cycle). Midostaurin 50 mg PO BID on days 8-21 continued per RATIFY protocol. Supportive medications: steroid eye drops (prednisolone acetate) for cytarabine-related conjunctivitis prophylaxis; prophylactic antimicrobials continued. Treatment intent: maintain remission and reduce MRD burden while awaiting transplant. One cycle planned prior to transplant given availability of 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor identified within 8 weeks.

At age 48 years (during HiDAC consolidation cycle 1), the patient developed Grade 2 cerebellar toxicity (ataxia, dysmetria) on day 6 of high-dose cytarabine, attributed to cytarabine neurotoxicity. Cytarabine held after 5 of 6 planned doses. Neurology consulted. Symptoms improved over 5 days with supportive care. No lasting neurologic deficit at time of discharge. Grade 3 neutropenia: ANC nadir 0/uL (day 14). No febrile neutropenia this cycle. Grade 2 mucositis: managed conservatively. Count recovery: ANC >1000/uL by day 28. No additional consolidation cycles administered; patient proceeded directly to pre-transplant evaluation given donor availability and acceptable toxicity profile.

At age 48 years (pre-transplant restaging, approximately 3.5 months after AML diagnosis), the patient had a PET-CT scan performed as pre-transplant restaging. Findings: No focal hypermetabolic lesions in lymph nodes, liver, spleen, or extramedullary sites. Mild residual splenomegaly (12.1 cm, improved from 14.2 cm at diagnosis). No evidence of chloroma or extramedullary AML. Bone marrow: mild heterogeneous FDG uptake pattern consistent with regenerating marrow post-chemotherapy. No abnormal skeletal lesions. Impression: No evidence of metabolically active disease. Compatible with complete remission. Radiology cancer status: no cancer identified on imaging.

At age 48 years (pre-transplant bone marrow biopsy, approximately 3.5 months after diagnosis), the patient had a repeat bone marrow biopsy performed at pre-transplant evaluation. Biopsy findings: Normocellular marrow (45% cellularity) with normal trilineage hematopoiesis. Myeloblasts less than 2% by morphology. Flow cytometry: No leukemia-associated immunophenotype detected (MRD NEGATIVE, sensitivity 0.01%). Cytogenetics: 46,XX [20/20]. FLT3-ITD by digital PCR: not detected (sensitivity < 0.01%). DNMT3A p.R882H by ddPCR: not detected. Conclusion: Sustained MRD-negative complete remission confirmed. Patient cleared for allogeneic stem cell transplant.

At age 48 years (approximately 4 months after AML diagnosis), the patient underwent matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT). Donor: 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1 allele-level match), CMV seronegative donor to CMV seropositive recipient (D-/R+ mismatch). Stem cell source: peripheral blood stem cells (PBSC), CD34+ cell dose 5.8 x 10^6/kg. Conditioning regimen: Reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) with Fludarabine 30 mg/m2/day IV on days -6 to -2 (5 days) plus Busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day IV on days -3 and -2 (2 days, IV busulfan with pharmacokinetic targeting, AUC 5,000 uM*min/day). GVHD prophylaxis: Tacrolimus (target trough 8-12 ng/mL) plus Methotrexate 15 mg/m2 on day +1, then 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, and +11. ATG (rabbit anti-thymocyte globulin, Thymoglobulin) 2.5 mg/kg/day IV on days -3, -2, -1. Midostaurin discontinued prior to conditioning. Transplant day 0: stem cells infused without acute infusion reaction. Supportive care: CMV surveillance by weekly PCR post-engraftment; PCP prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (or dapsone); antifungal prophylaxis with voriconazole; acyclovir for HSV/VZV prophylaxis; G-CSF held per institutional protocol post-PBSC transplant.

At age 48 years (day +18 post-alloHSCT), the patient had an inpatient transplant team assessment. Neutrophil engraftment confirmed (ANC >500/uL for 3 consecutive days, first noted day +16). Platelet engraftment: platelets >20,000/uL unsupported day +20. Overall engraftment: successful. No acute infusion reactions. No veno-occlusive disease/sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (VOD/SOS): bilirubin and transaminases within normal limits, no hepatomegaly or weight gain exceeding 5%. No acute GVHD skin rash, GI symptoms, or liver enzyme elevation meeting GVHD criteria (Grade 0 at this time). CMV PCR: negative. Patient tolerating oral tacrolimus and prophylactic medications. Performance status improving. Chimerism testing sent. Discharge planned. Cancer present: NO.

At age 48 years (day +30 post-alloHSCT, chimerism and MRD assessment), the patient had bone marrow biopsy and chimerism testing performed. Bone marrow biopsy: Normocellular marrow (50% cellularity), normal trilineage hematopoiesis, no morphologic evidence of AML relapse (blasts < 2%). Flow cytometry MRD: NEGATIVE. Donor chimerism (whole marrow): 98% donor by STR analysis. T-cell chimerism: 95% donor. Myeloid chimerism: 99% donor. FLT3-ITD by digital PCR: not detected. DNMT3A p.R882H by ddPCR: not detected. Impression: Successful full donor engraftment with MRD-negative complete remission confirmed at day +30. No evidence of graft failure or relapse.

At age 48 years (day +45 post-alloHSCT), the patient was found to have CMV reactivation. CMV PCR: 1,840 IU/mL (above institutional threshold of 250 IU/mL for pre-emptive treatment). Patient was asymptomatic (no CMV end-organ disease). Pre-emptive therapy initiated with Valganciclovir 900 mg PO BID. Serial weekly CMV PCR monitoring performed. CMV PCR became undetectable by day +60. Valganciclovir stepped down to prophylactic dosing (900 mg daily) and continued through day +100. No CMV end-organ disease developed. Tacrolimus levels stable; no dose adjustment required for CMV management.

At age 48 years (approximately day +90 post-alloHSCT), the patient developed signs and symptoms of mild chronic graft-versus-host disease (cGVHD). Manifestations: (1) Skin: lichenoid changes on the forearms and dorsal hands, mild pruritis, no ulceration or sclerosis (skin involvement ~15% BSA by modified Rodnan score); (2) Mouth: mild xerostomia and lichenoid changes of buccal mucosa, no ulcers, mild pain score 2/10; (3) Eyes: mild dry eye symptoms, Schirmer's test 7 mm (borderline). No liver, lung, GI, or musculoskeletal cGVHD. NIH Global Severity Score: Mild (1 organ with score 1, overall score 1). Tacrolimus taper held; dose maintained at therapeutic level. Topical clobetasol cream initiated for skin lesions. Oral dexamethasone rinse initiated for oral mucosal lesions. Ophthalmology referred; artificial tears and cyclosporine eye drops initiated. No systemic steroids required at this time. FEV1/FVC normal (no bronchiolitis obliterans).

At age 48 years (day +100 post-alloHSCT, 100-day evaluation), the patient had a formal 100-day transplant assessment. Overall status: excellent. Performance status ECOG 1. Chronic GVHD: mild, stable on topical therapies - skin lichenoid changes improving with topical clobetasol; oral mucosa improving with dexamethasone rinse; dry eyes managed with cyclosporine eye drops. No systemic immunosuppression beyond tacrolimus. CBC: WBC 4,800/uL (ANC 3,100/uL), Hgb 11.9 g/dL, platelets 168,000/uL. BMP: normal. Tacrolimus trough: 8.2 ng/mL. Repeat bone marrow biopsy deferred to day +180 per protocol given excellent clinical status. CMV PCR: negative (off valganciclovir prophylaxis as of day +100). PCP prophylaxis continued. Cancer present: NO. Remission status: MRD-negative CR1 maintained.

At age 48 years (day +180 post-alloHSCT, 6-month restaging), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast performed as 6-month post-transplant restaging. Findings: Normal spleen size (9.8 cm craniocaudal, resolved prior splenomegaly). No hepatomegaly. No lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusions. Lungs: clear bilaterally, no consolidation, no ground-glass opacities (no bronchiolitis obliterans pattern). No evidence of fungal infection or opportunistic pneumonia. No lytic or blastic bony lesions. No extramedullary disease. Impression: No evidence of AML relapse or extramedullary disease. Resolved splenomegaly. No pulmonary complications from transplant or GVHD. Radiology cancer status: no cancer identified.

At age 48 years (day +180 post-alloHSCT, 6-month bone marrow biopsy), the patient had a protocol bone marrow biopsy and aspirate. Biopsy findings: Normocellular marrow (40% cellularity), normal trilineage hematopoiesis, no morphologic evidence of AML. Myeloblasts less than 2% by morphology and flow cytometry. MRD by 8-color flow cytometry: NEGATIVE (sensitivity 0.01%). Donor chimerism: 99% donor (whole marrow), 98% donor (T-cells), 99% donor (myeloid). FLT3-ITD digital PCR: not detected. DNMT3A p.R882H ddPCR: not detected. ASXL1 frameshift ddPCR: not detected. Conclusion: Durable MRD-negative complete remission with full donor chimerism maintained at 6 months post-alloHSCT. Excellent molecular clearance of all tracked mutations.

At age 49 years (approximately 12 months post-alloHSCT, one-year transplant anniversary evaluation), the patient had a comprehensive annual transplant evaluation. Overall status: very good. Performance status ECOG 1. Chronic GVHD: mild, ongoing but improving - skin lichenoid changes nearly resolved (< 5% BSA); oral GVHD in remission (no active lesions, mild xerostomia persisting); dry eyes stable with eye drops. Tacrolimus successfully tapered to 1 mg daily (near discontinuation). No systemic steroids. No new organ involvement. CBC: WBC 5,600/uL, Hgb 12.8 g/dL, platelets 210,000/uL. BMP: normal. HbA1c: 6.8% (type 2 DM well-controlled on metformin resumed). Pulmonary function tests: FEV1 88% predicted, DLCO 82% predicted (mildly reduced DLCO, consistent with prior chemotherapy and/or GVHD, no obstructive pattern). Bone marrow biopsy: MRD negative, full donor chimerism (99% donor). Patient returned to part-time nursing work. Discussions: immunosuppression taper to completion planned over next 3 months; continued surveillance per transplant protocol. Vaccinations: inactivated vaccines resumed per post-transplant vaccination schedule. Cancer present: NO. Disease status: MRD-negative CR1, sustained 12 months post-alloHSCT.

At age 49 years (18 months post-alloHSCT), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast as surveillance imaging. Findings: No evidence of disease relapse. Normal organ sizes. No lymphadenopathy. No masses or lesions concerning for extramedullary AML. Lungs: clear. No pleural or pericardial effusion. Impression: No evidence of AML recurrence. Ongoing complete remission. Radiology cancer status: no cancer identified.

At age 49 years (18 months post-alloHSCT), the patient had a routine outpatient oncology/BMT follow-up visit. She had successfully completed tacrolimus taper and was fully off all immunosuppression as of month 15 post-transplant. Chronic GVHD: resolved by NIH criteria (no active manifestations requiring treatment; mild residual xerostomia managed with saliva substitutes and pilocarpine 5 mg TID). CBC: WBC 6,200/uL, Hgb 13.4 g/dL, platelets 234,000/uL - fully recovered counts. Peripheral NK cell and T-cell recovery confirmed by flow cytometry lymphocyte subset panel (CD4 count 380/uL, recovering appropriately). Metabolic panel: normal hepatic and renal function. Patient resumed full-time work. Bone marrow biopsy performed confirming ongoing MRD-negative remission with 99% donor chimerism. No signs of late effects (no avascular necrosis, no cataracts, no secondary malignancy on surveillance). Quality of life assessment (FACT-BMT): substantially improved compared to 6 months post-transplant. Next follow-up: 3 months (24-month evaluation). Cancer present: NO. Disease status: sustained MRD-negative CR1, 18 months post-alloHSCT, off all immunosuppression.

documents.text:
OUTPATIENT HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY CLINIC NOTE
Post-Induction Follow-Up Visit — Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML-MRC), FLT3-ITD High Allelic Ratio, ELN Adverse Risk

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 48-year-old woman with newly diagnosed AML with myelodysplasia-related changes (AML-MRC), FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, presenting for first outpatient follow-up visit following discharge from inpatient hospitalization after 7+3 induction chemotherapy plus midostaurin. She was discharged approximately 3 days ago after confirmation of morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity on day 30 bone marrow biopsy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 48-year-old LPN of Nigerian descent with a known antecedent history of myelodysplastic syndrome (RCMD) diagnosed approximately 18 months prior to the current presentation, which had been managed with observation. She initially presented roughly 5 weeks ago with a 2-week history of fatigue, progressive dyspnea on exertion, low-grade fevers, gingival bleeding, and easy bruising. At that time, her CBC was notable for WBC 84,000/uL with circulating blasts, hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL, and platelets 28,000/uL. Peripheral smear showed Auer rods. LDH was markedly elevated at 890 U/L. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed 62% CD34+ myeloblasts. Molecular workup revealed FLT3-ITD mutation at very high allelic ratio (VAF 0.82), along with co-mutations in DNMT3A (R882H), SRSF2, ASXL1, and RUNX1. Karyotype was broadly normal female (46,XX), though del(7q) was identified in a minor clone on FISH — consistent with the underlying MDS history. She was classified as ELN 2022 adverse-risk AML.

She was promptly admitted to the inpatient hematology unit and initiated on standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy: cytarabine 200 mg/m2/day as a continuous IV infusion on days 1–7 and daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 2, and 3, with midostaurin 50 mg PO BID added on days 8–21 per the RATIFY protocol given her FLT3-ITD mutation. Her inpatient course was complicated by febrile neutropenia on approximately day 10 with blood cultures growing E. coli (1 of 2 bottles), treated successfully with a 14-day course of cefepime with fever resolution. She also developed significant mucositis (WHO grade 3) requiring morphine PCA and a course of TPN for approximately 8 days. She required multiple pRBC and platelet transfusions throughout her aplastic phase. Transaminases were mildly elevated at peak (ALT 78 U/L) but self-resolved. No cardiac toxicity was observed.

A day-21 assessment showed no circulating blasts in the peripheral blood and her bone marrow biopsy was appropriately deferred given expected hypoplasia. Her day-30 restaging bone marrow biopsy showed a hypocellular marrow (25% cellularity) with regenerative trilineage hematopoiesis, blasts less than 2% by both morphology and flow cytometry, and — critically — MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry (sensitivity threshold 0.01%). Cytogenetics showed return to 46,XX in all 20 metaphases with no del(7q) clone. FLT3-ITD by PCR was undetectable. Morphologic complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity confirmed per ELN 2022 criteria.

She was discharged home approximately 3 days ago and presents today for her first outpatient visit. She reports feeling progressively better since discharge. She is eating well, tolerating oral medications without difficulty, and has had no fevers, chills, active bleeding, or new bruising. She notes mild fatigue and mild dyspnea with vigorous exertion, both significantly improved compared to her pre-treatment presentation. She denies chest pain, palpitations, or edema. She has been compliant with midostaurin 50 mg PO BID as directed. She denies nausea or GI intolerance with the midostaurin since the mucositis resolved.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Mild fatigue (improved from baseline pre-treatment), mild exertional dyspnea (improving), mild residual oral discomfort (resolving mucositis), mild xerostomia.
Negative: No fevers, no chills, no night sweats, no active bleeding or petechiae, no bruising, no epistaxis, no gingival bleeding, no nausea, no vomiting, no diarrhea, no constipation, no abdominal pain, no headache, no visual changes, no focal neurologic symptoms, no rash, no joint pain, no dysuria, no cough, no shortness of breath at rest, no chest pain, no lower extremity edema.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Acute myeloid leukemia with myelodysplasia-related changes (AML-MRC), FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN 2022 adverse risk — currently in morphologic CR1 with MRD negativity (diagnosed approximately 5 weeks ago)
2. Myelodysplastic syndrome (RCMD) — antecedent diagnosis approximately 18 months prior to AML diagnosis; managed with observation
3. Hypertension — managed with lisinopril
4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — managed with metformin (held during hospitalization, currently resumed)
No prior malignancies. No prior radiation therapy.

SOCIAL HISTORY
Ms. Okonkwo is a licensed practical nurse, currently on medical leave from her hospital position. She immigrated from Lagos, Nigeria approximately 20 years ago and is a permanent resident. She is married with two adult children. She is a lifelong never-smoker. She rarely consumes alcohol and denies illicit drug use. She has a supportive family structure; her husband has been accompanying her to appointments.

FAMILY HISTORY
No known family history of hematologic malignancy or leukemia. She reports a maternal aunt with breast cancer (postmenopausal, unknown ER/PR/HER2 status). Father had hypertension and died of a stroke. No known germline cancer predisposition syndromes in the family.

ALLERGIES
NKDA — no known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS
1. Midostaurin (Rydapt) 50 mg PO BID — FLT3 inhibitor, continuation per RATIFY protocol during consolidation planning phase
2. Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily — hypertension
3. Metformin 1000 mg PO BID — type 2 diabetes (resumed post-discharge)
4. Fluconazole 400 mg PO daily — antifungal prophylaxis
5. Acyclovir 400 mg PO BID — antiviral prophylaxis
6. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim DS) 1 tablet PO three times weekly — PCP prophylaxis
7. Ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN — nausea
8. Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily — GI prophylaxis
Note: Levofloxacin and allopurinol discontinued at discharge. Acyclovir and fluconazole maintained per post-discharge protocol.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Temperature 37.1°C, BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm and regular, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 71 kg (down approximately 4 kg from pre-admission baseline, expected post-induction).
General: Alert and oriented x3, pleasant and cooperative, mildly fatigued appearing but in no acute distress. Well-nourished.
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. Conjunctivae mildly pale. Oral mucosa: mild residual erythema of the buccal surfaces, no active ulceration, no significant mucositis lesion; teeth and gums appear healthy, no gingival bleeding appreciated on exam. No oral candidiasis.
Lymph nodes: No cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.
Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or rales. Good air entry.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly. Spleen tip barely palpable at the left costal margin (improved from prior exam). Bowel sounds normal and present in all four quadrants. No ascites.
Extremities: Warm, no edema, 2+ peripheral pulses. No rash. No petechiae or ecchymosis on the skin today.
Neurologic: Alert, fully oriented. Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Gait normal. No cerebellar findings.
Skin: No rash, no petechiae, no fungal skin lesions. PICC line site (right upper arm) appears clean, dry, and intact, without erythema or tenderness.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC (today):
  - WBC: 3,200/uL (differential: neutrophils 58%, lymphocytes 32%, monocytes 8%, eosinophils 2%, basophils 0%; no blasts on differential)
  - ANC: approximately 1,856/uL
  - Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL
  - MCV: 91 fL
  - Platelets: 142,000/uL
Comprehensive metabolic panel:
  - Na 139, K 4.0, Cl 102, CO2 25, BUN 14, Cr 0.88 mg/dL (eGFR > 90)
  - Glucose 108 mg/dL
  - ALT 32 U/L, AST 28 U/L, Alk Phos 74 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL — all normal, transaminase elevation fully resolved
  - Albumin 3.6 g/dL
LDH: 198 U/L (normalized from 890 at diagnosis)
Uric acid: 4.8 mg/dL (normal)
HbA1c: deferred (most recent pre-admission 6.9%)
Midostaurin trough level: not routinely monitored per protocol

Day +30 bone marrow biopsy results (performed prior to discharge, reviewed today):
  - Cellularity: 25%, regenerating
  - Myeloblasts: < 2% by morphology; < 1% by flow cytometry
  - MRD by 8-color flow cytometry: NEGATIVE (sensitivity 0.01%; no leukemia-associated immunophenotype)
  - Cytogenetics: 46,XX [20/20] — del(7q) clone undetected
  - FLT3-ITD by PCR: not detected
  - Diagnosis: Morphologic complete remission, MRD negative

---

IMAGING
Pre-treatment staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (performed at diagnosis): Mild splenomegaly at 14.2 cm, no extramedullary AML, no lymphadenopathy. No bony lesions.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. AML WITH MYELODYSPLASIA-RELATED CHANGES (AML-MRC), FLT3-ITD HIGH ALLELIC RATIO, ELN 2022 ADVERSE RISK — MORPHOLOGIC CR1, MRD NEGATIVE

Ms. Okonkwo has had an excellent response to 7+3 induction with midostaurin. Her day-30 bone marrow confirms morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity by sensitive flow cytometry — a very favorable result. Her peripheral counts are recovering nicely, with WBC 3,200, ANC approximately 1,856, Hgb 10.8, and platelets 142,000. I anticipate continued gradual count recovery over the next 2–4 weeks.

This is genuinely excellent news, but I want to be clear with the patient and family: given her disease characteristics — FLT3-ITD at high allelic ratio (VAF 0.82), co-mutations in DNMT3A R882H, SRSF2, ASXL1, and RUNX1, AML-MRC arising from antecedent MDS, and ELN adverse-risk classification — chemotherapy alone carries a very high relapse risk. Standard of care and current evidence strongly supports proceeding to allogeneic stem cell transplant (alloSCT) in first remission (CR1) for this patient profile. We discussed this at length with the patient and her husband today.

Plan:
  a. HLA typing for the patient has been initiated (already sent during inpatient stay). Sibling HLA typing results are pending — if no matched sibling is identified, we will pursue unrelated donor search via NMDP/Be The Match.
  b. BMT team referral placed today. They will see her in consultation this week to begin pre-transplant evaluation and planning, including GVHD risk counseling, goals of care discussion, and coordination with the donor registry.
  c. While awaiting donor identification and transplant logistics, the plan is to administer consolidation chemotherapy to maintain remission and further deepen MRD clearance. I anticipate high-dose cytarabine (HiDAC) as consolidation — the exact number of cycles will depend on how quickly a suitable donor becomes available. Midostaurin 50 mg BID will be continued per the RATIFY protocol during the consolidation phase.
  d. I explained to Ms. Okonkwo that the goal of transplant in this setting is cure — that alloSCT provides both myeloablation (eradicating residual leukemia) and a graft-versus-leukemia effect that chemotherapy alone cannot replicate, particularly important given her adverse molecular features.

2. MIDOSTAURIN CONTINUATION
Midostaurin 50 mg PO BID continued. Patient tolerating well without nausea or significant GI side effects at this time, with mucositis now resolved. No dose modification needed. Antiemetics available PRN. I reminded her to take midostaurin with a meal to improve tolerability.

3. RESOLVING MUCOSITIS AND NUTRITIONAL STATUS
Oral mucositis largely resolved; patient is eating well. Weight is down approximately 4 kg from pre-admission, but she reports appetite improving. Encourage high-calorie, high-protein diet. Continue oral hygiene regimen with chlorhexidine rinse and soft toothbrush. No active TPN or nutritional supplementation needed at this time.

4. ANEMIA AND THROMBOCYTOPENIA — RECOVERING
Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, platelets 142,000/uL — both recovering appropriately. No transfusion needed today. Recheck CBC in 1 week and as clinically indicated. I anticipate continued count recovery.

5. INFECTION PROPHYLAXIS
Continue fluconazole, acyclovir, and TMP-SMX prophylaxis per protocol. No fever or infectious symptoms at this time. Blood cultures not indicated. PICC line site clean.

6. DIABETES MANAGEMENT
Metformin 1000 mg BID resumed. HbA1c 6.9% most recently, well-controlled. Glucose today 108 mg/dL. Will recheck HbA1c at next visit. Counsel to continue home glucose monitoring given steroid and chemotherapy exposures anticipated. No dose changes needed today.

7. HYPERTENSION
BP 128/78 mmHg — well controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily. Continue current dose. Renal function normal (Cr 0.88).

8. PSYCHOSOCIAL
Ms. Okonkwo and her husband were given ample time for questions today. She was informed that the diagnosis of AML carries a serious prognosis but that her response to induction has been as good as we could hope for, and that transplant in first remission offers her a meaningful chance at long-term cure. She was emotional but composed. Social work referral placed for psychosocial support and practical assistance with medical leave documentation and insurance coordination. Patient is a nurse and has a good baseline understanding of her diagnosis; we spoke candidly about prognosis and goals.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 48-year-old Nigerian-American woman and licensed practical nurse with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and antecedent myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS, RCMD subtype, diagnosed approximately 18 months prior to AML transformation) who presented approximately 4 months ago with a new diagnosis of AML-MRC. At presentation, she had leukocytosis to 84,000/uL, severe anemia (Hgb 7.1), severe thrombocytopenia (platelets 28,000), and marked symptomatic disease including fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, gingival bleeding, and low-grade fevers. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed 62% myeloblasts with dysplastic features, and NGS demonstrated a high-allelic-ratio FLT3-ITD (VAF 82%) alongside co-mutations in DNMT3A (R882H), ASXL1, SRSF2, and RUNX1 — an adverse-risk genomic landscape per ELN 2022 criteria.

She received standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine 200 mg/m2/day days 1-7 continuous infusion plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV days 1-3) with midostaurin 50 mg BID added on days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol given FLT3-ITD positivity. Induction was complicated by E. coli bacteremia and febrile neutropenia managed successfully with cefepime, as well as grade 3 mucositis. Day +30 bone marrow biopsy confirmed morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry (sensitivity 0.01%), and FLT3-ITD was undetectable by PCR. She was discharged and continued midostaurin during the outpatient consolidation planning phase.

Given adverse-risk disease with multiple co-mutations and AML-MRC features, alloHSCT in CR1 was strongly recommended. HLA typing was initiated immediately; no matched sibling donor was available, and an unrelated donor search via the NMDP/Be The Match registry identified a 10/10 HLA allele-level matched unrelated male donor (CMV seronegative, D-/R+ mismatch) within approximately 8 weeks. Prior to transplant, she received one cycle of consolidation with high-dose cytarabine (HiDAC 3 g/m2 q12h days 1, 3, 5) plus midostaurin days 8-21. HiDAC was complicated by grade 2 cerebellar neurotoxicity (ataxia, dysmetria) on day 6, requiring early discontinuation after 5 of 6 planned doses, with full recovery without lasting neurologic deficit. Pre-transplant restaging PET-CT and repeat bone marrow biopsy at approximately 3.5 months from diagnosis confirmed sustained MRD-negative complete remission and no extramedullary disease; she was cleared for transplant.

Approximately 4 months after initial AML diagnosis, she underwent MUD alloHSCT with reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC): fludarabine 30 mg/m2/day IV on days -6 through -2 plus busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day IV on days -3 and -2 (IV busulfan with pharmacokinetic targeting, AUC ~5,000 uM*min/day). GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus (target trough 8-12 ng/mL), methotrexate (15 mg/m2 day +1, then 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, +11), and rabbit anti-thymocyte globulin (Thymoglobulin 2.5 mg/kg/day on days -3, -2, -1). Stem cells were peripheral blood stem cells with a CD34+ cell dose of 5.8 x 10^6/kg; infusion on day 0 proceeded without acute infusion reaction. Midostaurin was discontinued prior to conditioning.

She has been monitored daily since transplant. Today is day +18, and we are confirming engraftment. The past several days have been notable for gradual neutrophil recovery — ANC first exceeded 500/uL on day +16 and has remained above 500/uL for now 3 consecutive days, meeting the institutional definition of neutrophil engraftment. She has been afebrile for 5 days. She tolerated tacrolimus and prophylactic medications without significant adverse effects. She has no oral mucosal lesions, no skin rash, and no GI complaints. She denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal cramping. No right upper quadrant pain, no lower extremity edema, no significant weight change compared to baseline. She is eating well orally. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 48-year-old woman with AML-MRC, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN 2022 adverse risk, who achieved MRD-negative CR1 after 7+3 plus midostaurin, and who is now on day +18 following 10/10 MUD RIC-alloHSCT (flu/bu + ATG). Today's assessment is overall very encouraging.

1. NEUTROPHIL ENGRAFTMENT — CONFIRMED
ANC first exceeded 500/uL on day +16 and has remained above this threshold for 3 consecutive days, meeting institutional and standard transplant criteria for neutrophil engraftment. Engraftment day is recorded as day +16. This is a reassuring timeline for a RIC conditioning regimen with PBSC graft and ATG. Platelet engraftment anticipated within the next several days — current unsupported platelet count approaching 20,000/uL (platelet engraftment criterion: >20,000/uL unsupported for 3 consecutive days). Will continue to follow daily CBC. No interventions planned specifically for platelet engraftment other than continued supportive transfusion as needed (threshold 10,000/uL or <20,000/uL with active bleeding).

2. VENO-OCCLUSIVE DISEASE (VOD/SOS) — NO EVIDENCE
Liver function tests are entirely normal today (total bilirubin 0.9, ALT 28, AST 32, ALP 72). No hepatomegaly on exam. No right upper quadrant tenderness. No significant weight gain (< 0.5% above baseline). No ascites. Patient is not meeting any modified Seattle or EBMT criteria for VOD/SOS. Ursodiol 300 mg BID for prophylaxis is being continued through day +100. No defibrotide indicated at this time.

3. ACUTE GRAFT-VERSUS-HOST DISEASE (aGVHD) — GRADE 0 AT PRESENT
Careful daily assessment ongoing. Skin: no maculopapular rash, no erythema consistent with aGVHD. GI: no diarrhea, no nausea/vomiting of GVHD pattern, normal bowel pattern. Liver: LFTs normal, bilirubin normal. At this time there is no clinical evidence of acute GVHD in any organ system (grade 0 overall). It is important to note that with ATG-based GVHD prophylaxis and RIC conditioning, the time course of aGVHD may be somewhat delayed. We will remain vigilant. Tacrolimus trough is currently 9.4 ng/mL — within the therapeutic target range of 8-12 ng/mL. No dose adjustments needed today. Methotrexate GVHD prophylaxis doses are complete (last dose given on day +11).

4. INFECTIOUS DISEASE PROPHYLAXIS AND SURVEILLANCE
- CMV: Weekly CMV PCR surveillance initiated. Today's result is negative. D-/R+ CMV serostatus mismatch places this patient at moderate-to-high risk for CMV reactivation post-engraftment, particularly as immunosuppression continues. Will maintain weekly surveillance through at minimum day +100, per protocol. No pre-emptive therapy indicated at this time.
- Fungal prophylaxis: Voriconazole 200 mg PO BID ongoing; level therapeutic at 2.1 mg/L. Tacrolimus dose adjusted given CYP3A4 inhibition by voriconazole.
- Bacterial: Patient afebrile x 5 days. No active antibiotic therapy at present. Blood cultures from day +15 (sent in setting of low-grade fever to 38.1 degrees) with no growth; fever has resolved. No new infectious concerns today. Central line exit site clean. Will restart TMP-SMX (PCP prophylaxis) today given ANC now >1000/uL; single-strength three times weekly per protocol.
- HSV/VZV: Acyclovir 400 mg PO TID continued.
- No clinical or laboratory evidence of EBV-related lymphoproliferative process at this time; EBV PCR to be checked per protocol at day +30.

5. CHIMERISM TESTING
Whole-blood and lineage-specific chimerism (STR analysis) was sent on day +14 from peripheral blood; results pending. Bone marrow chimerism will be performed at the day +30 protocol biopsy. We anticipate high-level donor engraftment given robust and early recovery, PBSC graft source, and RIC conditioning. Results will be reviewed and discussed at day +30 assessment.

6. DISEASE STATUS / MRD MONITORING
Patient was in MRD-negative CR1 (by 8-color flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD digital PCR) at both the day +30 post-induction and pre-transplant bone marrow biopsies. No circulating blasts on current peripheral smear. Formal MRD assessment will be performed at the day +30 post-transplant bone marrow biopsy per protocol. Given the adverse-risk mutational profile (FLT3-ITD, DNMT3A, ASXL1, SRSF2, RUNX1), molecular MRD tracking including FLT3-ITD digital PCR and DNMT3A R882H ddPCR will be included at the day +30 biopsy.

7. FLUID STATUS / ELECTROLYTES
Mild bilateral lower extremity edema (1+) present, attributable to IV fluid administration during conditioning and early post-transplant period. Weight stable. No clinical or radiographic evidence of volume overload. Will reduce maintenance IV fluids and encourage oral hydration now that patient tolerating PO well. Magnesium mildly low (1.6 mEq/L) — continue oral magnesium supplementation; IV repletion if drops below 1.4 mEq/L. Phosphorus mildly low — oral replete as needed. Continue monitoring BMP daily.

8. GLYCEMIC MANAGEMENT
Glucose mildly elevated today (148 mg/dL) but acceptable. Insulin glargine 8 units at bedtime with sliding scale regular insulin. Metformin held during inpatient stay per standard protocol (contrast exposure risk, renal monitoring, low oral intake periods). Can revisit metformin reinstitution at discharge when renal function stable and patient reliably eating. HbA1c was 7.1% prior to transplant — reasonable control for a type 2 diabetic; anticipate glucose will be easier to manage once patient is ambulatory and eating full diet at home. Endocrine to be notified if persistent hyperglycemia.

9. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT
Patient is anxious but in good spirits overall, buoyed by the news of engraftment. Her husband is present and highly supportive. Social work has been involved throughout this hospitalization and will coordinate post-discharge support. Nurse navigator to discuss home care plan. Patient is a nurse herself and has been engaged in understanding her own care, which has been a strength throughout this illness.

10. DISCHARGE PLANNING
With neutrophil engraftment now confirmed and patient clinically stable, we are beginning active discharge planning. Target discharge within the next 2-3 days once platelet engraftment criterion is met (or near-met), patient is eating well, and no new complications arise. Patient and caregiver will receive education on:
- Daily temperature monitoring and when to call/present to clinic
- Central line care at home (Hickman)
- Tacrolimus administration, importance of consistent timing, and side effect recognition
- Medication adherence including antifungal, antiviral, and PCP prophylaxis
- Activity restrictions: avoid crowds and those with infections; food safety precautions (neutropenic diet can be liberalized gradually per institutional guidelines)
- Outpatient BMT clinic follow-up: within 2-3 days of discharge for CBC check, tacrolimus level, and wound check

OVERALL IMPRESSION: Day +18 post-MUD RIC-alloHSCT is proceeding very well. Neutrophil engraftment is confirmed as of day +16. No veno-occlusive disease, no acute GVHD, no CMV reactivation, no active infection. LFTs and renal function normal. Tacrolimus therapeutic. We are cautiously optimistic. Discharge anticipated within 2-3 days pending platelet engraftment and continued clinical stability.

Cancer present: NO. Disease status: MRD-negative CR1 maintained. Post-engraftment monitoring to continue per protocol.

I. BONE MARROW CORE BIOPSY (Specimen A) - Histologic Examination

This represents a significant and expected increase compared to the hypocellular marrow (25% cellularity) documented at day +30 of induction (post-7+3 chemotherapy), and is now approaching normal reconstitution consistent with successful engraftment of donor hematopoietic stem cells.

Hematopoietic lineages:
- Erythropoiesis: Normal maturation with erythroid precursors present in appropriate numbers and distribution. No dysplastic nuclear changes (no nuclear irregularity, megaloblastoid change, or binucleation exceeding expected background). Glycophorin A immunostain highlights erythroid islands distributed throughout the biopsy.
- Granulopoiesis: Active myeloid reconstitution observed. Normal left-shifted myeloid maturation with promyelocytes, myelocytes, metamyelocytes, and band forms identifiable. No significant dysplasia. MPO immunostain highlights maturing myeloid elements in normal distribution.
- Megakaryopoiesis: Megakaryocytes present in normal to slightly reduced numbers (approximately 3-5 per 10x field). Morphology is unremarkable without significant hypolobation, micromegakaryocytes, or clustering abnormalities that would suggest residual dysplasia.

Results:
- Whole bone marrow chimerism: 98% donor, 2% recipient
- T-cell subset chimerism (CD3+ sorted): 95% donor, 5% recipient
- Myeloid subset chimerism (CD33+ sorted): 99% donor, 1% recipient
- Peripheral blood chimerism: 97% donor, 3% recipient

Comment: Findings are consistent with near-complete donor engraftment at day +30 post-alloHSCT. Residual low-level recipient chimerism in the T-cell compartment (5%) is within expected range for day +30 following RIC conditioning with ATG, and does not indicate graft failure or impending rejection. Mixed T-cell chimerism at this early time point is commonly observed after RIC regimens, particularly with ATG-containing GVHD prophylaxis, and is expected to continue converting toward full donor chimerism over subsequent months. Sequential chimerism monitoring at day +60, +90, and +180 is recommended per protocol.

No evidence of increasing recipient contribution that would suggest impending graft failure or leukemic relapse of host origin.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
CORE BIOPSY HISTOLOGY:
The bone marrow core biopsy is markedly hypercellular, with an estimated overall cellularity of approximately 90-95% (markedly increased for patient age; expected normal cellularity approximately 45-55% for a 48-year-old). The marrow space is almost entirely replaced by hematopoietic elements with near-complete effacement of the normal fat spaces.

The dominant cellular population consists of medium-to-large immature mononuclear cells with irregular nuclear contours, finely dispersed to vesicular chromatin, prominent nucleoli (1-3 per cell), and moderate amounts of pale to light-blue cytoplasm. These cells are distributed diffusely throughout the marrow space and are also present in patchy aggregates. They constitute the overwhelming majority of the marrow cellularity and are morphologically consistent with myeloblasts.

Residual erythropoiesis is markedly reduced but identifiable; erythroid precursors show dysplastic features including nuclear irregularity, nuclear budding, occasional binucleation, and cytoplasmic bridging, consistent with dyserythropoiesis. Megaloblastoid changes are also noted in a subset of erythroid precursors.

Megakaryocytes are present but markedly decreased in number. The residual megakaryocytes display conspicuous dysplastic features: small hypolobated (monolobated and bilobated) forms predominate; micromegakaryocytes are identified; nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony is noted. No normal large, multilobated megakaryocytes are identified. These findings are consistent with dysmegakaryopoiesis.

Myeloid granulocytic precursors are markedly decreased, largely replaced by the blast population. The few residual maturing granulocytes show subtle dysplastic features including pseudo-Pelger-Huet nuclear hypolobation.

No lymphoid aggregates, granulomas, or metastatic tumor deposits are identified.

ASPIRATE MORPHOLOGY:
Aspiration yields cellular particles. A 500-cell differential count is performed on the aspirate smears:
- Myeloblasts (Type I and II): 62%
- Promyelocytes: 2%
- Myelocytes/Metamyelocytes: 3%
- Band/Segmented Neutrophils: 4%
- Monocytes: 2%
- Erythroid precursors (dysplastic): 15%
- Lymphocytes: 6%
- Plasma cells: 1%
- Other/Unclassifiable: 5%

The blasts (62%) are medium-to-large cells with a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, round to oval nuclei with finely granular chromatin, 1-3 distinct nucleoli, and moderate amounts of basophilic cytoplasm. Occasional cells display fine azurophilic granules in the cytoplasm. Importantly, Auer rods are identified in approximately 5-8% of blasts - these appear as thin, needle-like, magenta-staining cytoplasmic inclusions. The presence of Auer rods confirms the myeloid lineage of the blast population.

Dysplastic erythroid precursors (approximately 15% of cells) demonstrate nuclear irregularity, occasional nuclear bridging, multinucleation, and cytoplasmic vacuolization.

Dysplastic megakaryocyte fragments and micromegakaryocytes are identified on the aspirate smears.

PERIPHERAL BLOOD SMEAR:
Peripheral smear shows marked leukocytosis. Circulating blasts are identified, constituting approximately 35-40% of the differential white cell count. Blasts share morphologic features with marrow blasts, and Auer rods are identified in scattered peripheral blasts. Neutropenia with pseudo-Pelger-Huet cells noted. Normochromic normocytic anemia with anisopoikilocytosis. Severe thrombocytopenia with rare large platelets.

ANCILLARY STUDIES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (performed on core biopsy sections):
- CD34: Strongly and diffusely positive, highlighting the blast population; blasts constitute approximately 60-65% of total marrow cellularity by CD34 staining pattern.
- MPO (Myeloperoxidase): Positive in a subset of blasts (approximately 40-50%); strong positivity also in residual maturing granulocytes.
- CD117 (c-Kit): Positive in blasts.
- CD33: Positive (subset of blasts; confirmed by flow cytometry).
- TdT (Terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase): NEGATIVE (rules out lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma with myeloid differentiation).
- CD3: Negative in blasts.
- CD20: Negative in blasts.
- Lysozyme: Focally positive in blasts (consistent with myeloid lineage).
- Ki-67 (proliferation index): Approximately 70% in the blast population, consistent with a highly proliferative neoplasm.

FLOW CYTOMETRY IMMUNOPHENOTYPING (8-color multi-parameter panel, Specimen D):
Analysis performed on bone marrow aspirate. A distinct CD34-positive, CD45-low/intermediate blast gate is identified, comprising approximately 62% of total events.

Blast Immunophenotype:
- CD34: Positive (bright)
- CD117 (c-Kit): Positive
- CD33: Positive (bright)
- CD13: Positive
- HLA-DR: Positive
- CD123 (IL-3 receptor alpha): Positive
- CD7: Partially positive (aberrant T-cell antigen co-expression; present on approximately 30% of blasts)
- MPO (intracellular): Positive in subset (~45% of blasts)
- CD64: Dim positive (subset)
- CD14: Negative
- CD15: Negative (dim/partial on rare maturing cells)
- CD41: Negative
- CD61: Negative
- CD235a (Glycophorin A): Negative
- CD2: Negative
- CD3 (surface and cytoplasmic): Negative
- CD5: Negative
- CD19: Negative
- CD22: Negative
- CD10: Negative
- TdT: Negative
- Cytoplasmic IgM: Negative

Flow Cytometry Interpretation: Immunophenotypic profile is consistent with acute myeloid leukemia. The expression of CD34, CD117, CD33, CD13, HLA-DR, and CD123 with partial aberrant CD7 co-expression is a recognized immunophenotype in AML. MPO positivity confirms myeloid lineage commitment. The aberrant CD7 expression does not alter the primary diagnosis of AML. No immunophenotypic evidence of lymphoid or megakaryocytic differentiation.

CYTOGENETICS (G-banded karyotype, Specimen E):
Twenty metaphases analyzed.
Result: 46,XX [18/20]; del(7)(q22q36) [2/20]

Interpretation: The predominant clone (18/20 metaphases) demonstrates a normal female karyotype. Two of 20 metaphases harbor an interstitial deletion of the long arm of chromosome 7 [del(7q)]. This del(7q) clone is interpreted as representing a residual cytogenetically abnormal MDS-related subclone. No complex karyotype. No inv(16), t(8;21), t(15;17), t(9;11), or other recurrent AML-associated cytogenetic abnormalities identified. NOTE: Molecular studies are required for comprehensive risk stratification; cytogenetic results alone are insufficient to classify this AML.

FLUORESCENCE IN SITU HYBRIDIZATION (FISH) PANEL:
Panel performed on interphase nuclei from bone marrow aspirate (300 cells analyzed per probe set):
- FLT3 (13q12) ITD assay by fragment length analysis / FISH confirmation: FLT3-ITD POSITIVE. High allelic ratio detected (ITD:wildtype allele ratio approximately 0.82; ITD insert length approximately 39 base pairs, involving the juxtamembrane domain encoded by exons 14-15). NOTE: Quantitative allelic ratio was confirmed by molecular PCR-based fragment analysis performed concurrently.
- CBFB-MYH11 [inv(16)/t(16;16)]: NEGATIVE
- RUNX1-RUNX1T1 [t(8;21)]: NEGATIVE
- PML-RARA [t(15;17)]: NEGATIVE
- MLL (KMT2A) rearrangement (11q23): NEGATIVE
- MECOM (EVI1) rearrangement (3q26): NEGATIVE
- del(5q) / monosomy 5: NEGATIVE
- del(7q) / monosomy 7: POSITIVE - del(7q) signal pattern detected in 10% of cells analyzed (consistent with minor subclone, concordant with cytogenetic findings).
- del(17p) / TP53: NEGATIVE
- +8 (trisomy 8): NEGATIVE

MOLECULAR STUDIES (PCR-based, performed on bone marrow aspirate DNA):
- NPM1 exon 12 mutation (type A, B, C): WILD-TYPE (not detected)
- CEBPA mutation (single/double allele): WILD-TYPE (not detected)
- IDH1 (R132): WILD-TYPE (not detected)
- IDH2 (R140, R172): WILD-TYPE (not detected)

NOTE: Comprehensive next-generation sequencing (NGS) for additional somatic mutations (including DNMT3A, TET2, IDH1/2 expanded, RUNX1, ASXL1, SRSF2, SF3B1, and others) has been submitted as a separate order and results are PENDING at the time of this report. Results will be issued as an addendum.

RETICULIN STAIN:
Grade MF-1 (mild reticulin fibrosis; scattered reticulin fibers without intersections/crosslinks; mild increase above normal). No collagen fibrosis (MF-3). This degree of fibrosis is consistent with MDS-related changes and does not indicate primary myelofibrosis.

PAS STAIN:
Diffuse PAS positivity in blasts; block-positive PAS staining not identified (no lymphoblastic pattern). Consistent with myeloid blast lineage.

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Formalized 100-day post-allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant evaluation. Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 48-year-old woman with AML with myelodysplasia-related changes (AML-MRC), ELN 2022 adverse risk, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, now day +100 following 10/10 matched unrelated donor peripheral blood stem cell transplant (MUD PBSCT) with reduced-intensity conditioning. She presents today for comprehensive 100-day evaluation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo is well known to our BMT service. By way of brief summary: She is a 48-year-old Nigerian-American licensed practical nurse with a prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome (RCMD, diagnosed approximately 18 months before her current leukemia diagnosis) who presented with leukocytosis (WBC 84,000/uL), severe anemia, thrombocytopenia, and constitutional symptoms and was found to have AML with myelodysplasia-related changes per the 2022 WHO classification. Diagnostic bone marrow biopsy confirmed 62% CD34-positive myeloblasts. Cytogenetics showed a predominantly normal 46,XX karyotype with a minor del(7q) clone (residual MDS-related clone in 2/20 metaphases). Comprehensive NGS panel revealed a high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD (VAF 0.82), DNMT3A p.R882H, ASXL1 frameshift, SRSF2 p.P95H, and RUNX1 p.R204Q -- a mutational landscape consistent with AML arising from antecedent MDS and placing her firmly in the ELN adverse-risk category. Pre-treatment imaging showed mild splenomegaly without extramedullary disease; echocardiogram demonstrated a normal EF of 62%, clearing her for anthracycline-based therapy.

She received standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine 200 mg/m2 continuous infusion x7 days plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3) with midostaurin 50 mg BID on days 8-21, per the RATIFY protocol. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia with E. coli bacteremia, Grade 3 mucositis requiring morphine PCA and temporary TPN, and significant transfusion requirements, all of which resolved. Day +30 bone marrow biopsy confirmed morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD undetectable by PCR -- an excellent and gratifying response to induction.

Given her adverse-risk disease profile, allogeneic stem cell transplant in CR1 was strongly pursued. While awaiting donor identification, she received one cycle of HiDAC consolidation (cytarabine 3 g/m2 q12h on days 1, 3, 5) plus midostaurin, which was complicated by Grade 2 cerebellar neurotoxicity after five of six planned doses of cytarabine; cytarabine was held and she recovered neurologically without lasting deficit. Pre-transplant bone marrow evaluation confirmed sustained MRD-negative CR1 with complete molecular clearance of FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A mutations by digital PCR. PET-CT imaging at that time showed no evidence of extramedullary or metabolically active disease.

She underwent 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (male donor, CMV D-/R+ serostatus mismatch) peripheral blood stem cell transplant with a reduced-intensity conditioning regimen consisting of fludarabine/busulfan (Flu/Bu2 RIC), with rabbit ATG (Thymoglobulin) for additional T-cell depletion, and GVHD prophylaxis with tacrolimus plus short-course MTX. She received a CD34+ cell dose of 5.8 x 10^6/kg. Stem cells infused on day 0 without acute reaction.

Post-transplant course:
- Engraftment: Neutrophil engraftment confirmed on day +16 (ANC >500 for 3 consecutive days). Platelet engraftment on day +20. Day +30 bone marrow biopsy showed 98% donor chimerism (whole marrow), 99% myeloid chimerism, 95% T-cell chimerism, MRD negative, no evidence of relapse.
- CMV reactivation: Detected on day +45 (CMV PCR 1,840 IU/mL), asymptomatic, treated pre-emptively with valganciclovir 900 mg BID; CMV PCR became undetectable by day +60, valganciclovir stepped down to prophylactic dosing and continued through day +100.
- Chronic GVHD (day +90): Patient developed NIH global mild chronic GVHD with skin lichenoid changes on the forearms and dorsal hands (~15% BSA), mild oral mucosal lichenoid changes with xerostomia, and borderline dry eyes (Schirmer's 7 mm). No hepatic, pulmonary, GI, or musculoskeletal cGVHD. Tacrolimus taper was held; topical clobetasol initiated for skin, oral dexamethasone rinse for oral mucosa, and cyclosporine eye drops plus artificial tears initiated for ocular involvement. No systemic corticosteroids were required.

Today, on day +100, she comes in for her formal 100-day evaluation. Overall, she reports feeling progressively better. She is living at home with family support. She notes mild residual fatigue, which has improved significantly over the past 4 weeks. She rates her overall energy at 6/10 compared to 4/10 six weeks ago. The skin lichenoid changes on her forearms and hands are improving; she is applying clobetasol ointment twice daily as directed and notes decreased pruritus. Oral dryness and mild oral discomfort persist but are less bothersome -- she is using the dexamethasone rinse and notes improvement in the mucosal irritation. Dry eyes remain mildly symptomatic; she is using cyclosporine drops and artificial tears frequently. No dysphagia, odynophagia, or difficulty opening her mouth. No abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. No evidence of GI GVHD symptomatology. No cough, dyspnea, or wheeze (no pulmonary GVHD symptoms). No jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, or weight change suggesting hepatic GVHD. No joint pain or stiffness. No muscle cramps or weakness.

She remains on tacrolimus, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (PCP prophylaxis; now off valganciclovir), acyclovir, and her outpatient medications including lisinopril and metformin. CMV PCR has been negative since day +60. She was successfully taken off valganciclovir as of today (day +100) per institutional protocol.

No recent hospitalizations. No new infections. No bleeding. Appetite improved; she has gained approximately 3 lbs over the past 4 weeks.

CLINICAL INDICATION

The patient is a 48-year-old female with a history of acute myeloid leukemia with myelodysplasia-related changes (AML-MRC), per 2022 WHO classification, arising from antecedent myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS, RCMD subtype). The patient harbored an adverse-risk mutational profile at diagnosis including FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio (VAF 0.82), DNMT3A p.R882H, ASXL1 p.G646Wfs*12, SRSF2 p.P95H, and RUNX1 p.R204Q, with cytogenetic findings of 46,XX in the dominant clone and a minor del(7q) subclone. She achieved morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity following induction with 7+3 (cytarabine 200 mg/m2/day x7 days plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2/day x3 days) with midostaurin. She subsequently received one cycle of high-dose cytarabine (HiDAC) consolidation followed by matched unrelated donor allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (10/10 HLA-matched, male unrelated donor, peripheral blood stem cells, CD34+ dose 5.8 x 10^6/kg) with reduced-intensity conditioning (fludarabine/busulfan, pharmacokinetic-targeted IV busulfan) and GVHD prophylaxis consisting of tacrolimus, mini-dose methotrexate, and rabbit anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG, Thymoglobulin).

This bone marrow biopsy is a protocol 6-month post-alloHSCT restaging evaluation (approximately day +180). Clinical status at time of biopsy: patient is well-appearing, ECOG performance status 1, CBC showing WBC 4,800/uL, Hgb 11.9 g/dL, platelets 168,000/uL, with no circulating blasts on differential. She has mild chronic GVHD (NIH Global Severity: Mild) involving skin (nearly resolved on topical clobetasol), oral mucosa (resolving on dexamethasone rinse), and dry eyes (managed with cyclosporine eye drops). Tacrolimus trough: 8.2 ng/mL. CMV PCR: negative.

Clinical questions: (1) Is AML in sustained morphologic complete remission? (2) What is the MRD status by multiparameter flow cytometry? (3) What is the donor chimerism status? (4) Are there any morphologic or cytogenetic features concerning for early relapse, graft failure, or secondary myeloid neoplasm?

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 49-year-old Nigerian-American woman presenting for her formal one-year post-allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT) evaluation. She is doing well overall, has returned to part-time nursing work, and comes in today for protocol-mandated surveillance, comprehensive laboratory assessment, pulmonary function testing, and bone marrow biopsy to confirm ongoing remission status.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo was originally diagnosed with acute myeloid leukemia with myelodysplasia-related changes (AML-MRC) arising from a known antecedent myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS with refractory cytopenia and multilineage dysplasia, identified approximately 18 months prior to AML diagnosis). At diagnosis she presented with a WBC of 84,000/uL, hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL, platelets 28,000/uL, and 62% myeloblasts on bone marrow biopsy — a quite dramatic presentation. Molecular workup revealed a FLT3-ITD mutation with high allelic ratio (VAF 0.82), along with co-mutations in DNMT3A (R882H), ASXL1 (G646Wfs*12), SRSF2 (P95H), and RUNX1 (R204Q). Cytogenetics showed a normal female karyotype with a minor del(7q) clone remnant from the antecedent MDS. By ELN 2022 criteria this placed her firmly in the adverse-risk category, with allogeneic transplant in first remission as the clear treatment goal.

She received 7+3 induction chemotherapy with cytarabine and daunorubicin plus midostaurin (per RATIFY protocol) and achieved a morphologic and MRD-negative complete remission (CR) by day 30 bone marrow biopsy. Her induction course was complicated by febrile neutropenia with E. coli bacteremia (successfully treated with cefepime for 14 days), Grade 3 mucositis requiring morphine PCA and TPN support for approximately 8 days, and Grade 3 anemia/thrombocytopenia requiring transfusion support — all expected in the setting of myeloablative-intent 7+3. Midostaurin was well tolerated without dose reduction.

She went on to receive one cycle of high-dose cytarabine (HiDAC, 3 g/m2 q12h on days 1, 3, and 5) as consolidation, complicated by Grade 2 cerebellar ataxia and dysmetria after 5 of 6 planned doses (classic cytarabine neurotoxicity), with full neurologic recovery. Given prompt identification of a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor, she proceeded directly to transplant after one consolidation cycle. Pre-transplant restaging confirmed sustained MRD-negative remission both by PET-CT (no hypermetabolic disease) and bone marrow biopsy (MRD negative, FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A not detected by ddPCR).

Approximately four months after AML diagnosis, she underwent matched unrelated donor peripheral blood stem cell transplant (MUD alloHSCT) with a 10/10 HLA-matched CMV-seronegative male donor (she was CMV seropositive, D-/R+ mismatch). Conditioning: reduced-intensity with fludarabine/busulfan (Flu/Bu2, pharmacokinetic-targeted IV busulfan, AUC ~5,000 uM*min/day). GVHD prophylaxis: tacrolimus plus MTX (days +1, +3, +6, +11) plus rabbit ATG (Thymoglobulin) on days -3, -2, -1. Engraftment was prompt: ANC nadir recovery to >500/uL by day +16, platelets unsupported >20,000/uL by day +20. Day +30 chimerism showed 99% donor myeloid, 95% donor T-cell, with MRD-negative marrow.

Early post-transplant course was notable for two significant complications:
1. CMV reactivation at day +45 (peak PCR 1,840 IU/mL in a D-/R+ setting), treated pre-emptively with valganciclovir 900 mg BID with clearance by day +60, then stepped down to prophylaxis through day +100. No CMV end-organ disease developed.
2. Mild chronic GVHD emerging around day +90: lichenoid skin changes on the forearms and dorsal hands (~15% BSA), oral mucosa lichenoid changes with xerostomia, and borderline Schirmer's test (7 mm) reflecting mild dry eye syndrome. NIH Global Severity Score: mild. Managed with topical clobetasol for skin, dexamethasone oral rinse for mucosa, and cyclosporine eye drops plus artificial tears. Tacrolimus taper was held. No systemic steroids were required. At the day +100 evaluation, counts were fully recovering (WBC 4,800/uL, Hgb 11.9 g/dL, platelets 168,000/uL), chronic GVHD was stable and improving, and MRD remained negative.

At the 6-month evaluation (day +180), bone marrow biopsy confirmed ongoing MRD-negative complete remission, full donor chimerism (99% whole marrow, 98% T-cell, 99% myeloid), and molecular clearance of FLT3-ITD, DNMT3A R882H, and ASXL1 frameshift mutations by ddPCR. CT imaging showed resolved splenomegaly and no evidence of extramedullary disease.

Today she presents for the formal 12-month/one-year evaluation. She reports she has been doing really well. She returned to part-time nursing work about two months ago and is tolerating it without excessive fatigue. Her skin lichenoid changes have nearly resolved. She still notices some dryness of the mouth, particularly at night, but she is managing with saliva substitutes and pilocarpine (started at a prior visit). Her eye dryness has been adequately controlled with cyclosporine eye drops and artificial tears. She denies any new skin rashes, GI symptoms, dyspnea, or joint stiffness. She has had no fevers, infections, or hospitalizations since her last clinic visit 3 months ago. Her blood sugars have been well-controlled on metformin (recently rechecked HbA1c 6.8%), and her blood pressure has been acceptable on lisinopril. She feels her energy and quality of life have substantially improved compared to the first few months post-transplant.

No new complaints. Tacrolimus taper has been ongoing per protocol — she is currently on 1 mg daily, nearly at the point of discontinuation, which is excellent given her mild cGVHD picture.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 49-year-old woman with a history of adverse-risk AML-MRC (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, with DNMT3A/ASXL1/SRSF2/RUNX1 co-mutations, arising from antecedent MDS) who underwent 7+3 plus midostaurin induction with achievement of MRD-negative CR1, followed by one cycle of HiDAC consolidation, and then 10/10 MUD alloHSCT with reduced-intensity Flu/Bu conditioning. She is now at 12 months post-transplant and is doing exceptionally well by every objective and subjective measure.

1. AML-MRC, MRD-negative CR1 — 12 months post-alloHSCT
This is the most important item. Today's bone marrow biopsy confirms ongoing morphologic and molecular remission with MRD negativity by sensitive flow cytometry and ddPCR for all three tracked mutations (FLT3-ITD, DNMT3A R882H, ASXL1 G646Wfs*12, SRSF2 P95H). Full donor chimerism maintained at 99% across all lineages. CBC is entirely normal. This is the clinical picture we were hoping for — this patient with adverse-risk disease has now achieved one year of sustained MRD-negative remission post-transplant. The graft-versus-leukemia effect is likely contributing to this durable remission. She will continue protocol surveillance. No AML-directed therapy indicated or planned at this time.

Cancer present: NO. Disease status: Sustained MRD-negative CR1, 12 months post-alloHSCT.

2. Chronic GVHD — Mild, Resolving
By NIH global severity scoring, her chronic GVHD is now mild-to-resolving: skin involvement is < 5% BSA with only faint residual pigmentation changes (inactive by exam), oral involvement is in remission with only residual xerostomia managed symptomatically with pilocarpine and saliva substitutes, and ocular cGVHD is stable on topical cyclosporine drops. She has not required systemic steroids, which is reassuring and avoids the well-known metabolic and immunosuppressive complications of long-course steroid therapy. Given the excellent trajectory, the plan is to continue tacrolimus taper to 0.5 mg daily over the next 4-6 weeks, then discontinue entirely if she remains stable. Topical clobetasol can be tapered and discontinued given near-complete resolution of skin lesions. Oral dexamethasone rinse: continue PRN only. Pilocarpine: continue for xerostomia. Ophthalmology follow-up scheduled in 3 months.

3. Immunosuppression Taper and Prophylaxis Management
Tacrolimus 1 mg daily — taper to 0.5 mg daily over the next 4-6 weeks, then discontinue, barring any cGVHD flare.
PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX): CD4 count now 380/uL, above the 200/uL threshold for safe discontinuation. Given she is approaching complete discontinuation of tacrolimus and CD4 recovery is progressing, PCP prophylaxis may be safely discontinued after tacrolimus is fully stopped and she has been off immunosuppression for at least 4 weeks. Plan to discuss at 15-month visit.
Acyclovir: Continue through at least 18-24 months post-transplant per institutional protocol (VZV reactivation risk remains elevated).
No antifungal prophylaxis currently — appropriate given immune recovery and CD4 > 200/uL.
CMV and EBV PCR: both undetectable at 12 months, no active monitoring needed at monthly intervals; will check at 15-month visit.

FEV1/FVC ratio is normal (0.77), ruling out bronchiolitis obliterans (the most feared pulmonary complication in the context of cGVHD). Mildly reduced DLCO at 82% predicted, likely reflecting a combination of residual chemotherapy effect (anthracycline, busulfan), prior conditioning-related injury, and/or mild subclinical pulmonary cGVHD. No clinical symptoms of dyspnea at rest. Will monitor DLCO at next formal PFT assessment (planned at 18-month evaluation). No intervention indicated. She should be encouraged to continue aerobic reconditioning, as this will help improve her functional capacity.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 49-year-old Nigerian-American woman and licensed practical nurse who presents today for her 18-month post-alloHSCT surveillance evaluation. She was diagnosed approximately 22 months ago with AML-MRC arising from antecedent MDS, presenting with a WBC of 84,000/uL, hemoglobin of 7.1 g/dL, and severe thrombocytopenia (platelets 28,000/uL), with 62% blasts on bone marrow biopsy and FLT3-ITD mutation identified at high allelic ratio (VAR 0.82). NGS at diagnosis additionally revealed co-mutations in DNMT3A p.R882H, ASXL1 p.G646Wfs*12, SRSF2 p.P95H, and RUNX1 p.R204Q — a mutational landscape conferring ELN 2022 adverse-risk classification and strongly favoring consolidative alloSCT in CR1.

She received standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy with cytarabine 200 mg/m2 continuous infusion over days 1-7 and daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 on days 1 through 3, with midostaurin 50 mg BID added on days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol given her FLT3-ITD mutation. Her induction course was complicated by E. coli bacteremia with febrile neutropenia (treated successfully with 14 days of cefepime), grade 3 mucositis requiring TPN support, and transfusion dependence, all of which resolved. Day +30 bone marrow biopsy confirmed morphologic complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry and undetectable FLT3-ITD by PCR.

She received one cycle of high-dose cytarabine (HiDAC) consolidation at 3 g/m2 q12h on days 1, 3, and 5, which was complicated by grade 2 cerebellar toxicity — ataxia and dysmetria — developing on day 6, requiring early discontinuation after 5 of 6 planned doses. Neurotoxicity resolved fully without long-term sequelae.

Approximately 4 months after diagnosis, she underwent a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor alloHSCT (PBSC, CD34+ dose 5.8 x 10^6/kg) using reduced-intensity conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan. GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus, mini-methotrexate (days +1, +3, +6, +11), and rabbit ATG. Neutrophil engraftment occurred at day +16, and day +30 bone marrow biopsy confirmed full donor chimerism (98% whole marrow, 99% myeloid) with MRD negativity.

Post-transplant course was notable for CMV reactivation at day +45 (peak PCR 1,840 IU/mL), treated pre-emptively with valganciclovir 900 mg BID with clearance by day +60, without end-organ disease. She also developed mild chronic GVHD at approximately day +90 with skin lichenoid changes (~15% BSA), oral mucosal involvement (lichenoid changes, xerostomia), and borderline dry eye symptoms (Schirmer's 7 mm). NIH global severity was mild (score 1). This was managed topically with clobetasol cream, oral dexamethasone rinse, and cyclosporine eye drops, without the need for systemic corticosteroids.

At the 12-month anniversary evaluation, she was doing very well — chronic GVHD was improving, tacrolimus was being tapered, and bone marrow biopsy confirmed ongoing MRD-negative remission with full donor chimerism. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at 18 months was recently performed and showed no evidence of disease relapse or extramedullary AML. She had successfully completed her tacrolimus taper and was fully off all immunosuppression by approximately month 15 post-transplant.

Today, she reports no fevers, chills, or night sweats. She notes mild persistent dry mouth, which she manages with saliva substitutes and pilocarpine. She returned to full-time nursing work approximately 2 months ago and tolerates her work schedule without difficulty. She denies bleeding, bruising, or new lymphadenopathy. No respiratory symptoms, no GI complaints, no joint stiffness or skin changes suggesting recrudescent GVHD. She endorses mild fatigue at end of workday, which she attributes to her demanding schedule. No bone pain. No headaches or visual changes. Libido somewhat reduced (she acknowledges this but declines hormonal evaluation at this time).

She takes metformin 1000 mg BID (type 2 DM, HbA1c 6.8% at last check, well-controlled) and lisinopril 10 mg daily (hypertension, well-controlled at home). She resumed all post-transplant vaccinations per protocol. She notes excellent compliance with saliva substitutes, pilocarpine, and artificial tear drops.
